Seq Scan: Postgres walks the table from the first row to the last and checks each one. No index is used. Fine for small tables or when most rows are needed anyway — an index wouldn't help much in that case.
Index Scan: Postgres walks the index to find matching entries, then fetches the actual row data from the table for each one. Works well when few rows match — but each table fetch is a separate disk jump.
Index Only Scan: Like Index Scan, but no table access needed — all required data is already in the index itself. The fastest option when the index covers all requested columns.
Bitmap Index Scan: First step of a two-phase read. Postgres scans the index and builds a map of which table pages contain matching rows. The table itself is not read yet — only the pages are marked.
Bitmap Heap Scan: Second step after Bitmap Index Scan. Postgres takes the page map and reads the table — each needed page exactly once. This is faster than jumping around the table randomly as in a plain Index Scan.
BitmapAnd: Takes two bitmap maps from different indexes and keeps only pages present in both. This is AND — a row must satisfy both conditions. Allows two indexes to be used at once.
BitmapOr: Takes two bitmap maps and merges them — a page is included if it appears in either one. This is OR — a row satisfies at least one of the conditions.
Tid Scan: Direct row fetch by its physical address (ctid). Rarely seen — only when you explicitly write WHERE ctid = ... in your query. Very fast, but almost never used in regular queries.
Tid Range Scan: Like Tid Scan but for a range of physical addresses. Added in PostgreSQL 16. Used with WHERE ctid BETWEEN ... AND ..., for example to read different chunks of a table in parallel.
Subquery Scan: A wrapper around a subquery — Postgres executes it and passes the result up as a regular table. Appears when the subquery cannot be merged directly into the plan, e.g. when it contains LIMIT or ORDER BY.
Function Scan: Calls a function that returns a set of rows and exposes them as a table. Typical for generate_series(), unnest(), and any other set-returning functions used in FROM.
Table Function Scan: Similar to Function Scan but used with ROWS FROM(...) syntax — when a function is explicitly declared as a row source with defined columns.
Values Scan: Reads rows from a constant VALUES(...) list written directly in the query. Nothing is fetched from anywhere — the data is already in the query itself.
Sample Scan: Reads a random sample of rows from a table using TABLESAMPLE. Handy when exactness is not required and you want a quick statistical estimate on a large table without a full scan.
CTE Scan: Reads the result of a WITH expression (CTE) that was computed and stored beforehand. By default Postgres materializes CTEs — computes them once and caches the result, even if that is not optimal.
Named Tuplestore Scan: Reads rows from an internal buffer that Postgres uses to pass data between parts of the plan. Mostly seen in parallel queries and window functions — rarely visible in simple queries.
WorkTable Scan: Reads the intermediate result of the current recursion step in WITH RECURSIVE. On each iteration it takes the rows produced by the previous step and feeds them into the recursive part of the query.
Foreign Scan: Accesses an external data source through a FDW (Foreign Data Wrapper) — for example another Postgres, a file, or a remote API. What actually happens inside depends on the specific FDW.
Custom Scan: A scan node added by a third-party extension — for example Citus or TimescaleDB. Postgres hands control to the extension, which reads data in its own way.
Nested Loop: For each row from one table, Postgres looks for matching rows in the other. Works well when few rows are involved or when there is an index on the inner table. Without an index on large data — slow.
Merge Join: Joins two tables that are pre-sorted on the join key — walks through them in parallel like a zipper. If the data is not sorted yet, Postgres sorts it first before joining.
Hash Join: Takes the smaller table, builds a hash table from it in memory, then scans the larger table and looks up matches. Works well on large unsorted data but requires memory.
Aggregate: Computes aggregate functions: COUNT, SUM, AVG, MAX, MIN and others. Can group rows (GROUP BY) either by sorting or via a hash table — Postgres chooses based on the data.
Group: Groups already-sorted rows without any aggregation. Rarely seen — appears when a query has GROUP BY but no aggregate functions.
WindowAgg: Computes window functions (OVER(...)). Works on already-sorted data — so there is almost always a Sort node before it in the plan. Each window function in the query gets its own WindowAgg node.
Unique: Removes duplicates from already-sorted rows. Used for DISTINCT and UNION (without ALL). Simple and fast — just compares adjacent rows one by one.
SetOp: Performs set operations: INTERSECT, INTERSECT ALL, EXCEPT, EXCEPT ALL. Works on sorted data — walks through two row sets in parallel and compares them.
Sort: Sorts rows. If there is enough memory — sorts in RAM, otherwise spills to temporary disk files. Does not return any rows to the parent node until all rows are sorted.
Incremental Sort: Smart sort: if the data is already partially ordered (e.g. by leading sort keys), it only sorts within each group. Faster than a plain Sort and starts returning rows sooner.
Hash: Builds a hash table from rows for the parent Hash Join. This is the build side of the join — data is loaded into memory and will later be probed for matches.
Materialize: Saves the child node's output in memory (or on disk) so it can be read multiple times. Needed for example when the same table is the inner side of a Nested Loop and gets scanned repeatedly.
Memoize: Caches child node results by their parameters. If the same call with the same parameters was already made — returns the cached result instead of re-executing. Especially useful inside Nested Loop.
Gather: Collects rows from multiple parallel workers into a single stream. This is the point where a parallel query converges back into one process. Row order is not guaranteed.
Gather Merge: Like Gather, but order-preserving — merges already-sorted streams from workers into one sorted stream. Used when the output of a parallel plan needs to be sorted.
Append: Simply concatenates rows from multiple sources one after another. This is the basis for UNION ALL and for reading partitioned tables — Postgres reads each partition separately and concatenates the results.
Merge Append: Like Append but order-preserving — merges already-sorted results from multiple sources into one sorted stream. Used when reading partitions with a required ORDER BY to avoid an extra sort step.
Recursive Union: Implements WITH RECURSIVE. First executes the base query, then repeatedly applies the recursive part to the result of the previous step — until no more rows are produced.
Limit: Stops execution as soon as the required number of rows is collected (LIMIT) or skips the first N rows (OFFSET). Postgres does not read any further data than necessary.
Result: Returns a result without accessing any table. Used for SELECT without FROM — for example SELECT 1+1 or SELECT now(). Sometimes appears as an intermediate node for expression evaluation.
ProjectSet: Expands set-returning functions used directly in the SELECT list. If you write SELECT unnest(array) — this node appears: it takes one input row and generates multiple output rows from it.
LockRows: Locks the rows returned by the query — FOR UPDATE or FOR SHARE. After this, other transactions cannot modify those rows until the current transaction ends.
ModifyTable: The root node of any data modification: INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE. Receives rows from child nodes and applies changes to the table, firing triggers if any exist.
Insert: Inserts rows into a table. A child node of ModifyTable. Rows can come from VALUES, a subquery, or any other source.
Update: Updates rows in a table. A child node of ModifyTable. Receives old row versions, computes new column values, and writes them.
Delete: Deletes rows from a table. A child node of ModifyTable. Rows are not removed physically right away — they are marked as dead and cleaned up later by VACUUM.
